title: Daily Current Affairs Roundup
content: A daily overview of major developments in policy, economy, international relations and science.

title: India–Israel Ties: From Tactical Engagement to Structural Partnership
content:
India–Israel relations have evolved from cautious engagement to a deep strategic partnership across defence, technology, agriculture and innovation. India recognized Israel in 1950, while full diplomatic ties were established in 1992.

Trust strengthened during the Kargil War (1999), when Israel provided critical military supplies. In 2017, relations were elevated to a Strategic Partnership, marking deeper cooperation.

Today, collaboration spans AI, cybersecurity, space, agriculture and water technology. India imports nearly 34% of Israeli arms exports, while trade reached $3.62 billion in 2024-25. New initiatives in critical technologies and innovation highlight the partnership’s growing geopolitical and economic significance.

title: Transforming Indian Agriculture: From Production Powerhouse to Value-Driven Growth Engine
content:
Indian agriculture, valued at nearly $600 billion, supports close to 45% of India’s workforce while contributing about 15–18% to Gross Value Added (GVA). Despite being a global leader in foodgrain, milk, fruit, and vegetable production, India captures only 2.5–3% of the $8 trillion global agri-food trade. Low value addition (~10%) means most produce is exported as raw commodities, highlighting the urgent need for a market-linked, value-chain driven transformation.

Current Developments Driving Agricultural Growth

Digital Public Infrastructure (AgriStack & Bharat-VISTAAR): India is building a sovereign digital backbone for agriculture, reducing information asymmetry and enabling precision governance. With over 8.48 crore Farmer IDs and 28.5 crore plots mapped, this system enables targeted subsidies and real-time market linkages. The Union Budget 2026–27 introduced Bharat-VISTAAR, a multilingual AI advisory platform integrating ICAR insights.

PM Dhan-Dhaanya Krishi Yojana: Launched with an outlay of ₹24,000 crore, this initiative targets 100 low-productivity districts, benefiting 1.7 crore farmers through localized, data-driven interventions aimed at crop diversification and resilience.

Aatmanirbharta in Pulses & Oilseeds: With ₹11,440 crore allocation, this mission seeks import independence by promoting domestic cultivation and ensuring guaranteed procurement of key crops like tur, urad, and masoor.

Agriculture Infrastructure Fund (AIF): Providing 3% interest subvention, AIF has sanctioned ₹80,224 crore across 1.5 lakh projects, boosting storage, processing, and reducing post-harvest losses.

AI & Precision Farming: Tools like Kisan e-Mitra and Krishi Decision Support System deliver real-time advisory, handling over 8,000 daily queries. Drone deployment and AI pest surveillance are modernizing farming practices.

Climate-Resilient Insurance: Backed by ₹69,515 crore, tech-driven insurance systems like YES-TECH and WINDS enable faster claim settlements, benefiting millions of farmers.

Decentralized Storage & Cooperatives: PACS modernization is enabling village-level storage infrastructure, with 70,000 MT capacity already developed in pilot phases.

Horticulture & Export Diversification: Focus on high-value crops like makhana, cashew, and cocoa is driving export-oriented growth and reducing monoculture risks.

Key Challenges in Indian Agriculture

Climate Volatility: Extreme weather impacted crops on 331 out of 334 days in 2025, damaging 17.4 million hectares.

Land Fragmentation: Average landholding is just 1.08 hectares, with 86% farmers small or marginal, limiting mechanization.

Soil Degradation: Skewed fertilizer use (N:P:K ratio of 10.9:4.1:1) is degrading soil health.

Water Stress: Agriculture consumes 80% of freshwater, yet micro-irrigation remains limited.

Market Distortions: MSP bias toward cereals discourages diversification; India still imports 16 million tonnes of edible oil.

Supply Chain Inefficiencies: Post-harvest losses cost ₹92,651 crore annually, with perishables losing up to 30%.

Credit Gaps: Marginal farmers remain dependent on informal lending despite schemes like MISS.

Way Forward: Making Agriculture Profitable

High-Value Crop Transition: Moving toward bio-economy crops like medicinal plants and cocoa can boost farm incomes.

AgriStack-Based Financing: Linking land, data, and credit can enable precision lending and reduce dependency on moneylenders.

Solar-Powered Infrastructure: Decentralized cold storage and processing units can reduce distress sales.

AI-Driven Advisory: Platforms like Bharat-VISTAAR can reduce input costs by up to 20%.

FPO Strengthening: Farmer collectives can bypass intermediaries and capture higher value.

Climate-Resilient Seeds: Genome-edited crops ensure yield stability amid climate shocks.

Drone-as-a-Service: Precision spraying reduces costs and improves efficiency.

Allied Sector Integration: Livestock and fisheries provide diversified, stable income streams.

Conclusion:
India must transition from a "Green Revolution" to a "Green-Gold Revolution" by integrating digital infrastructure, climate-resilient technologies, and decentralized value chains. Empowering smallholders through data, diversification, and collective market power will be key to unlocking sustainable agricultural profitability.
